FT_IC1.evidence: Data on screen time, ADHD symptoms, and brain structure extracted from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study; n=10,116 at baseline, ages 9-10

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear mixed-effects models with baseline and 2-year follow-up data; mediation analysis examining brain structure as mediator

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Translational Psychiatry (2025)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as total time on devices including phone/video games, with weighted daily score (weekday/weekend); paired with ADHD symptoms (Child Behavior Checklist, individual-trait behavioral outcome)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: screen time → ADHD symptoms β=0.032, p=0.001; screen time → cortical thickness (right temporal pole β=-0.036, FDR p=0.020); mediation effect β=0.001, p=0.023

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results for SM/phone ↔ outcome relationship: β coefficients, FDR-corrected p-values, effect sizes (d=0.045); mediation pathway estimates

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design, but screen time is the IV (predictor) of ADHD symptoms and brain structure; not SM-as-DV-only. Paper kept.
notes: Strong study with large sample, clear phone/screen time measurement, robust analysis of longitudinal associations with both neural and behavioral outcomes

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study; abstract identifies ABCD cohort with Hispanic and non-Hispanic participants

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: structural equation modeling (SEM) and t-tests examining relationships between caffeine intake, screen time, and sleep outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Sleep Advances (2025)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measurement: weekday/weekend evening screen time; paired with individual-trait-level outcomes including chronotype, social jet lag, weekday sleep duration, and psychological factors (perceived stress, depression, anxiety)

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results: screen time associated with later chronotype and greater social jet lag (Cohen's d=0.27 weekday, d=0.35 weekend); SEM model variance explained: R²=0.129 (chronotype), R²=0.105 (social jet lag), R²=0.062 (weekday sleep)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantifications: effect sizes (Cohen's d), SEM model R² values, associations with sleep outcomes reported

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 rule applies only to longitudinal analyses. Paper kept.
notes: No PDF; assessed from abstract. Screen time clearly paired with individual-trait sleep and psychological outcomes. Study includes demographic stratification (Hispanic vs. non-Hispanic)

FT_IC1.evidence: Cohort study of ABCD Study data from baseline through year 4 follow-up (2016-2022); population-based samples from 21 US sites; n=4,285 in analytic sample

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: latent class linear mixed models to identify addictive use trajectories (years 2-4); Poisson regression for categorical outcomes; generalized linear models for continuous outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in JAMA (2025); major journal

FT_IC2.evidence: Validated addictive use questionnaires: 6-item SMAQ (social media), 8-item MPIQ (mobile phones), 6-item VGAQ (video games); all qualify as SM/phone variables. Paired with individual-trait-level outcomes: suicidal behaviors, suicidal ideation, internalizing symptoms (CBCL), externalizing symptoms (CBCL)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results for trajectories ↔ outcomes: social media high-peaking RR=2.39 (95% CI 1.66-3.43) for suicidal behaviors; increasing RR=2.14 (95% CI 1.61-2.85); CBCL internalizing T-score differences (e.g., video game high trajectory mean diff=2.03, 95% CI 1.45-2.61)

FT_IC4.evidence: Comprehensive quantitative results: risk ratios with 95% CIs, mean differences with 95% CIs, FDR-corrected p-values across suicidal behaviors, ideation, and mental health outcomes

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design, but SM/phone addictive use is the IV (predictor of suicidal behaviors and mental health outcomes), not DV. Paper kept.
notes: Large population-based longitudinal study; platform-specific measurement (social media, mobile phone, video games assessed separately); strong prospective design with multiple mental health outcomes
